title: A Demonstration of Quantum Circuit Implementation for Obstacle Flow Using Carleman-Linearized Lattice Boltzmann Method
authors: Kazumasa Ueno, Keita Kanno, Yasunori Lee
abstract: Fluid simulations, especially at high Reynolds numbers, are computationally expensive on classical computers, making them promising application targets for quantum computing. Recent studies have combined the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) with Carleman linearization to design quantum algorithms for computational fluid dynamics (CFD). However, practical quantum-circuit implementations of these algorithms that incorporate non-periodic boundary conditions have not been fully explored. In this work, we implement a quantum algorithm for two-dimensional linearized fluid flow around an obstacle, using block-encoding of the linear-system matrix and quantum singular value transformation (QSVT) to solve it. Inflow, outflow, and no-slip boundary conditions are formulated as sparse matrix operations and efficiently embedded into quantum circuits using index-value encoding. We demonstrate logarithmic scaling of the required numbers of qubits and gates with respect to the number of lattice points, suggesting the potential feasibility of quantum-computational fluid dynamics simulations.
summary: This paper presents a quantum algorithm for simulating 2D fluid flow around obstacles by linearizing the Lattice Boltzmann Method via Carleman linearization. It provides a detailed gate-level implementation of boundary conditions and demonstrates that the quantum computational cost scales logarithmically with spatial resolution.
main_problem: Developing a practical quantum-circuit implementation for Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) that incorporates non-periodic boundary conditions and obstacle geometries.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a gate-level implementation of the Carleman-linearized Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) showing logarithmic scaling of qubits and gates with respect to spatial resolution.
method: The approach uses Carleman linearization to transform nonlinear fluid equations into a linear system, which is then solved using Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) and block-encoding.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional fluid flow around a rectangular obstacle using the D2Q9 Lattice Boltzmann Model with the BGK collision operator.
key_observables: Fluid density, velocity field, and the effective condition number of the linear system.
important_parameters: Reynolds number (Re), Mach number (Ma), relaxation time (tau), number of lattice points (N), and Carleman truncation order (Nc).
main_assumptions: The method assumes first-order Carleman linearization, weakly compressible flow, and a low Mach number regime.
figures_summary: Flowcharts of the linearization pipeline, illustrations of the D2Q9 velocity vectors, gate-level circuit architectures for block-encoding and QSVT, and velocity field snapshots from simulations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantum CFD, details the Carleman-LBM linearization, describes the quantum circuit construction (block-encoding and QSVT), presents resource estimation and scaling analysis, and concludes with validation against classical references.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
